evals.name: manager-injects-tracking-repo
evals.expected_output: Should flag two violations: (1) Manager persists (calls InsertAsync/UpdateAsync) — domain services must not persist; (2) Manager injects tracking IRepository<T> instead of IReadOnlyRepository<T>. Corrected version should: use IReadOnlyRepository<Customer/Order>; have CreateAsync return the new Order without persisting; have ChangePriceAsync take Order as a parameter and mutate in place; show the AppService minting the Id via GuidGenerator and persisting via tracking IRepository.
assertions: Flags that the Manager calls InsertAsync / UpdateAsync (i.e. persists), and states this is wrong because domain services must not persist
Recommends switching the Manager's injected repositories to IReadOnlyRepository<T, Guid> (for Customer and Order)
Corrected ChangePriceAsync receives an Order as a parameter from the caller rather than loading it via _orderRepository.GetAsync(orderId) inside the Manager
Corrected CreateAsync returns the new Order without calling InsertAsync inside the Manager
Shows an OrderAppService that orchestrates: holds the tracking IRepository<Order, Guid>, mints Id via GuidGenerator, calls the Manager, and persists with InsertAsync/UpdateAsync

evals.name: appservice-uses-iqueryable
evals.expected_output: Should reject calling GetQueryableAsync from inside the AppService (Rule 2: never expose IQueryable to the application service layer). Recommend defining a custom IOrderRepository : IRepository<Order, Guid> in the Domain project, and an EF Core implementation (OrderRepository : EfCoreRepository<...>, IOrderRepository) in the EntityFrameworkCore project. The custom repo encapsulates the LINQ query and exposes a method like GetActiveForCustomerAsync(...) that returns materialised results (List<Order> or PagedResultDto). AppService injects IOrderRepository and calls it. Bonus: mention IAsyncQueryableExecuter for provider-neutral async LINQ inside the custom repo.
assertions: Flags that calling GetQueryableAsync (or composing IQueryable / LINQ) inside the AppService is wrong
Recommends defining a custom repository interface (e.g. IOrderRepository : IRepository<Order, Guid>) in the Domain project
Shows the EF Core implementation of that custom repository (e.g. inheriting EfCoreRepository<...> in the EntityFrameworkCore project) where the IQueryable / LINQ logic actually lives
Refactored AppService injects the custom repository and calls a method that returns materialised results (List<Order> / PagedResultDto / count + list), with no GetQueryableAsync call remaining

Registers the custom repository through ABP's repository pipeline — e.g. options.AddRepository<Order, OrderRepository>() inside the EF Core module's AddAbpDbContext<>() configuration, or via the auto-registration that follows from inheriting EfCoreRepository<,,> in the same EF Core project. Does NOT register via generic services.AddScoped<IOrderRepository, OrderRepository>() / IServiceCollection extension methods (an ABP-specific DI lock-in)

evals.name: cross-module-read
evals.prompt: I'm working in an ABP 10.3 Layered modular monolith. My `Acme.Reports` module needs to read 'how many submissions a given owner has' from the `Acme.Forms` module to build a report. The Forms module already has a `FormSubmissionAppService` with this query. Can I just inject `IFormSubmissionAppService` into my `ReportAppService`? What's the right pattern?
evals.expected_output: Should reject directly injecting IFormSubmissionAppService across modules. Recommend ABP Integration Services: define IFormSubmissionIntegrationService in Acme.Forms.Application.Contracts marked with [IntegrationService], implement it in Acme.Forms.Application as FormSubmissionIntegrationService : ApplicationService, IFormSubmissionIntegrationService. Reports module references Forms.Application.Contracts and injects the integration service interface. Should mention: hidden from public API, audit-off by default, no [Authorize] by default. Should explain that in modular-monolith both modules share the host so it resolves in-process; in microservice the same interface is exposed over /integration-api and consumed via a generated proxy. Should NOT recommend a host-level shared type or directly injecting IFormSubmissionAppService.
assertions: Recommends against directly injecting the public IFormSubmissionAppService across module boundaries
Recommends ABP Integration Services as the right pattern, explicitly mentioning the [IntegrationService] attribute
Defines the integration-service interface in the producing module's *.Application.Contracts (or *.Contracts) project
Shows the implementation in the producing module's *.Application project (or equivalent), inheriting ApplicationService and implementing the [IntegrationService] interface
Notes that integration services are hidden from the public API surface (and/or audit-off by default / not [Authorize] by default)
Explains the in-process modular-monolith vs microservice transport difference (DI in-process now vs HTTP / generated proxy across services), framed as 'same contract, different transport'

Specifies that the consumer module's AbpModule depends only on the producer's *.Application.Contracts module (e.g. [DependsOn(typeof(AcmeFormsApplicationContractsModule))] on the Reports.Application module) — NOT on the producer's full *.Application module. This contracts-only dependency is the load-bearing detail that preserves the module boundary

evals.name: controller-on-integration-service
evals.expected_output: Should reject the hand-written controller. ABP exposes [IntegrationService] interfaces over its own integration-services pipeline (typically /integration-api/...). The two-class explicit-controller pattern (Rule 4) does NOT apply to integration services — ABP wires HTTP exposure implicitly. Should recommend deleting FormSubmissionIntegrationController. Bonus: mention that integration services are audit-off and have no [Authorize] by default — adding [Authorize] on the controller defeats the design and the implicit pipeline handles security boundaries differently. The interface and the implementation are correct.
assertions: Tells the user to delete (or remove) the hand-written FormSubmissionIntegrationController

Explains that ABP wires the integration-service HTTP exposure implicitly (via the integration-services pipeline / /integration-api / ExposeIntegrationServices / ApplicationServiceTypes.IntegrationServices), so writing a controller is unnecessary or wrong
Confirms that the [IntegrationService] interface and its ApplicationService implementation (the first two blocks) are correct as written

Calls out that integration services are not meant to live on the public /api surface (any of: hidden from public API, audit-off, no [Authorize] by default — i.e. mismatched with what the controller is doing)

evals.name: add-module-dbcontext
evals.prompt: I'm adding a new `Acme.Billing` module to my existing ABP 10.3 Layered solution (it's a modular monolith with two existing modules already). The host runs against PostgreSQL. Walk me through everything I need to set up on the EF Core side so migrations work correctly and don't conflict with the other modules. Show me the key files I need to write.
evals.expected_output: Should produce: (1) BillingDbContext : AbpDbContext<BillingDbContext> in the Billing.EntityFrameworkCore project. (2) A design-time IDesignTimeDbContextFactory<BillingDbContext> alongside the DbContext so `dotnet ef migrations add` works. (3) Wire AddAbpDbContext<BillingDbContext>(...) and Configure<AbpDbContextOptions> with options.UseNpgsql(b => b.MigrationsHistoryTable("__EFMigrationsHistory_Billing")) — the unique history table name is critical to avoid EF re-applying other modules' migrations. (4) An IBillingDbSchemaMigrator interface (Domain.Shared) and EntityFrameworkCoreBillingDbSchemaMigrator implementation that calls _dbContextProvider.GetDbContext().Database.MigrateAsync(). (5) The host's DbMigrator picks up the new IBillingDbSchemaMigrator via the loop over all registered migrators. Should mention separate Migrations folder under the module's EFCore project. Should NOT suggest sharing a single DbContext across modules.
assertions: Recommends a separate BillingDbContext owned by the Billing module (does NOT recommend sharing/extending the host's existing DbContext)

Specifies a unique EF Core migrations history table for this module — explicitly something like __EFMigrationsHistory_Billing — and explains why (avoid EF treating other modules' migrations as missing)
Includes an IDesignTimeDbContextFactory<BillingDbContext> implementation alongside the DbContext, explaining it's needed for `dotnet ef migrations add` (or equivalent)

Includes a per-module schema migrator: an IBillingDbSchemaMigrator interface and an EntityFrameworkCoreBillingDbSchemaMigrator (or equivalently named) implementation that runs migrations on the module's DbContext
Wires the DbContext with the PostgreSQL provider (UseNpgsql) — i.e. the answer is template-aware about Postgres, not a generic SQL Server example

evals.name: controller-injecting-repo
evals.expected_output: Should reject and recommend the explicit two-class pattern (Rule 4): split into OrderAppService : ApplicationService, IOrderAppService (owns the manager, repository, ObjectMapper, current user, and CreateAsync orchestration) and OrderController : MyBaseController, IOrderAppService (constructor injects ONLY IOrderAppService; action body is a single delegating expression). Hard prohibitions to call out: (a) controller must not inject anything other than IOrderAppService — no IRepository, no OrderManager, no IObjectMapper, no ICurrentUser for business decisions; (b) declarative [Authorize(Policy="...")] / [Authorize(Roles="sales")] on the action, not imperative role checks via ICurrentUser. Bonus: mention that ApplicationService already provides ObjectMapper / CurrentUser / GuidGenerator so the AppService doesn't need to inject them.
assertions: Recommends moving the orchestration (manager call + persist + map) out of the controller and into an OrderAppService that the controller delegates to

States the controller should inject only the application-service interface (e.g. IOrderAppService) and that injecting IRepository / OrderManager / IObjectMapper / ICurrentUser into a controller is wrong

Replaces the imperative _currentUser.IsInRole("sales") check with declarative [Authorize(...)] referencing an ABP permission constant (e.g. [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] or similar) — NOT [Authorize(Roles="sales")] (role strings) and NOT a bare [Authorize] without a policy/permission
Shows the corrected action body as a single delegating expression to the AppService (or a one-liner equivalent) — no business logic in the controller

Applies [Authorize(OrderPermissions.Create)] (or equivalent permission-based attribute) at the application-service layer — on the AppService class or the CreateAsync method itself — NOT only on the controller. The AppService is the canonical authz boundary in ABP because other callers (background jobs, integration services, event handlers) routing through the AppService also get the check

evals.name: businessexception-in-appservice
evals.expected_output: Should reject all the BusinessException throws inside the AppService (Rule 7) and split them by where the rule actually lives.
assertions: States the principle that BusinessException throws should not live inside the AppService, and that input/invariant rules belong elsewhere

Moves the input-format validations (Number required / max length) onto the DTO via DataAnnotations (e.g. [Required], [StringLength(32)]) or FluentValidation — i.e. routes them through ABP's validation pipeline
Moves the cross-aggregate uniqueness check (existing OrderNumber) into a domain-layer concept — a *Manager / domain service (or onto the entity) — not the AppService
Moves the Customer.IsBlocked guard into either an entity-behavior method (e.g. customer.EnsureCanReceiveNewOrder()) or the OrderManager — not the AppService
Refactored OrderAppService no longer contains throw new BusinessException(...) statements; it orchestrates (call manager, persist, map) only

Domain-rule violations (uniqueness check, IsBlocked guard, customer-not-found) are signalled by throwing BusinessException (or UserFriendlyException / EntityNotFoundException) from the Manager / entity / entity-behavior method — keeps ABP's exception-based error model. Does NOT replace the throws with a functional Result<T,E> / Either / discriminated-union return type, and does NOT swallow them silently
